content:
# Genesis Chapter 23 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folio 12v–13r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_023_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_023_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 18–19 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0022

---

*The Death of Sarah — Kiriath-arba / Hebron (vv. 1–2)*

**23:1** And the life of Sarah [וַיִּהְיוּ חַיֵּי שָׂרָה] was one hundred years [מֵאָה שָׁנָה] and twenty years [וְעֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה] and seven years [וְשֶׁבַע שָׁנִים] — the years of the life of Sarah [שְׁנֵי חַיֵּי שָׂרָה].

**23:2** And Sarah died [וַתָּמָת שָׂרָה] in Kiriath-arba [בְּקִרְיַת אַרְבַּע] — which is Hebron [הִוא חֶבְרוֹן] — in the land of Canaan [בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן]. And Abraham came [וַיָּבֹא אַבְרָהָם] to mourn for Sarah [לִסְפֹּד לְשָׂרָה] and to weep for her [וְלִבְכֹּתָהּ].

---

*Abraham's Petition to the Hittites — "A Sojourner and a Resident" (vv. 3–9)*

**23:3** And Abraham arose [וַיָּקָם אַבְרָהָם] from before his dead [מֵעַל פְּנֵי מֵתוֹ] and spoke [וַיְדַבֵּר] to the sons of Heth [אֶל-בְּנֵי-חֵת] saying [לֵאמֹר]:

**23:4** A sojourner and a resident [גֵּר-וְתוֹשָׁב] am I [אָנֹכִי] with you [עִמָּכֶם] — give to me [תְּנוּ לִי] a holding of a burial-place [אֲחֻזַּת-קֶבֶר] with you [עִמָּכֶם] and I will bury [וְאֶקְבְּרָה] my dead [מֵתִי] from before my face [מִלְּפָנָי].

**23:5** And the sons of Heth answered [וַיַּעֲנוּ בְנֵי-חֵת] Abraham [אֶת-אַבְרָהָם] saying [לֵאמֹר] to him:

**23:6** Listen to us [שְׁמָעֵנוּ] — my lord [אֲדֹנִי] — a prince of God [נְשִׂיא אֱלֹהִים] are you [אַתָּה] in our midst [בְּתוֹכֵנוּ]. In the choicest [בְּמִבְחַר] of our burial places [קְבָרֵינוּ] bury [קְבֹר] your dead [מֵתֶךָ]. Not one among us [אִישׁ מִמֶּנּוּ] will withhold [יִכְלֶה] from you [מִמְּךָ] his burial place [אֶת-קִבְרוֹ] from burying [מִקְּבֹר] your dead [מֵתֶךָ].

**23:7** And Abraham arose [וַיָּקָם אַבְרָהָם] and bowed down [וַיִּשְׁתַּחוּ] to the people of the land [לְעַם-הָאָרֶץ] — to the sons of Heth [לִבְנֵי-חֵת].

**23:8** And he spoke with them [וַיְדַבֵּר אִתָּם] saying [לֵאמֹר]: If it is in your soul [אִם-יֵשׁ אֶת-נַפְשְׁכֶם] to bury [לִקְבֹּר] my dead [אֶת-מֵתִי] from before my face [מִלְּפָנָי] — listen to me [שְׁמָעוּנִי] and intercede for me [וּפִגְעוּ-לִי] with Ephron [בְּעֶפְרוֹן] the son of Zohar [בֶּן-צֹחַר].

**23:9** And he will give to me [וְיִתֶּן-לִי] the cave of Machpelah [אֶת-מְעָרַת הַמַּכְפֵּלָה] that is his [אֲשֶׁר-לוֹ] — which [אֲשֶׁר] is at the end of his field [בִּקְצֵה שָׂדֵהוּ]. For the full silver [בְּכֶסֶף מָלֵא] let him give it to me [יִתְּנֶנָּה לִי] in your midst [בְּתוֹכְכֶם] as a holding of a burial-place [לַאֲחֻזַּת-קָבֶר].

---

*Ephron's Offer and the Negotiation (vv. 10–16)*

**23:10** And Ephron [וְעֶפְרוֹן] was sitting [יֹשֵׁב] in the midst of the sons of Heth [בְּתוֹךְ בְּנֵי-חֵת] — and Ephron the Hittite answered [וַיַּעַן עֶפְרוֹן הַחִתִּי] Abraham [אֶת-אַבְרָהָם] in the hearing of the sons of Heth [בְּאָזְנֵי בְנֵי-חֵת] — to all the entering-ones of the gate of his city [לְכֹל בָּאֵי שַׁעַר-עִירוֹ] — saying [לֵאמֹר]:

**23:11** No, my lord [לֹא-אֲדֹנִי] — listen to me [שְׁמָעֵנִי] — the field [הַשָּׂדֶה] I give to you [נָתַתִּי לָךְ] and the cave [וְהַמְּעָרָה] that is in it [אֲשֶׁר-בּוֹ] — to you [לְךָ] I give it [נְתַתִּיהָ] — before the eyes of the sons of my people [לְעֵינֵי בְנֵי-עַמִּי] — I give it to you [נְתַתִּיהָ לָּךְ] — bury your dead [קְבֹר מֵתֶךָ].

**23:12** And Abraham bowed down [וַיִּשְׁתַּחוּ אַבְרָהָם] before the people of the land [לִפְנֵי עַם הָאָרֶץ].

**23:13** And he spoke to Ephron [וַיְדַבֵּר אֶל-עֶפְרוֹן] in the hearing of the people of the land [בְּאָזְנֵי עַם-הָאָרֶץ] saying [לֵאמֹר]: Surely if only you [אַךְ אִם-אַתָּה] — listen to me [לוּ שְׁמָעֵנִי] — I give the silver for the field [נָתַתִּי כֶּסֶף הַשָּׂדֶה] — take it from me [קַח מִמֶּנִּי] and I will bury [וְאֶקְבְּרָה] my dead [אֶת-מֵתִי] there [שָׁמָּה].

**23:14** And Ephron answered [וַיַּעַן עֶפְרוֹן] Abraham [אֶת-אַבְרָהָם] saying [לֵאמֹר] to him:

**23:15** My lord [אֲדֹנִי] — listen to me [שְׁמָעֵנִי] — a land of four hundred shekels of silver [אֶרֶץ אַרְבַּע מֵאֹת שֶׁקֶל-כֶּסֶף] — between me and you [בֵּינִי וּבֵינְךָ] — what is that [מַה-הִוא]? And your dead [וְאֶת-מֵתְךָ] — bury [it] [קְבֹר].

**23:16** And Abraham listened to [וַיִּשְׁמַע אַבְרָהָם] Ephron [אֶל-עֶפְרוֹן] and Abraham weighed out [וַיִּשְׁקֹל אַבְרָהָם] for Ephron [לְעֶפְרֹן] the silver [אֶת-הַכֶּסֶף] that he had spoken [אֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר] in the hearing of the sons of Heth [בְּאָזְנֵי בְנֵי-חֵת] — four hundred shekels of silver [אַרְבַּע מֵאוֹת שֶׁקֶל כָּסֶף] — passing [currency] for the merchant ['עֹבֵר לַסֹּחֵר].

---

*The Legal Transfer of Property (vv. 17–18)*

**23:17** And the field of Ephron [וַיָּקָם שְׂדֵה עֶפְרוֹן] that was in Machpelah [אֲשֶׁר בַּמַּכְפֵּלָה] — which is before Mamre [אֲשֶׁר לִפְנֵי מַמְרֵא] — the field [הַשָּׂדֶה] and the cave [וְהַמְּעָרָה] that was in it [אֲשֶׁר-בּוֹ] and every tree [וְכָל-הָעֵץ] that was in the field [אֲשֶׁר בַּשָּׂדֶה] — that was in all its boundary [אֲשֶׁר בְּכָל-גְּבֻלוֹ] round about [סָבִיב] — arose [confirmed] [וַיָּקָם]

**23:18** to Abraham as a possession [לְאַבְרָהָם לְמִקְנָה] before the eyes of the sons of Heth [לְעֵינֵי בְנֵי-חֵת] — among all the entering-ones of the gate of his city [בְּכֹל בָּאֵי שַׁעַר-עִירוֹ].

---

*The Burial of Sarah (vv. 19–20)*

**23:19** And after this [וְאַחֲרֵי-כֵן] Abraham buried [קָבַר אַבְרָהָם] Sarah his wife [אֶת-שָׂרָה אִשְׁתּוֹ] in the cave of the field of Machpelah [אֶל-מְעָרַת שְׂדֵה הַמַּכְפֵּלָה] — before Mamre [עַל-פְּנֵי מַמְרֵא] — which is Hebron [הִוא חֶבְרוֹן] — in the land of Canaan [בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן].

**23:20** And the field [וַיָּקָם הַשָּׂדֶה] and the cave [וְהַמְּעָרָה] that was in it [אֲשֶׁר-בּוֹ] arose [confirmed] [וַיָּקָם] to Abraham [לְאַבְרָהָם] as a holding of a burial-place [לַאֲחֻזַּת-קָבֶר] from the sons of Heth [מֵאֵת בְּנֵי-חֵת].

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**On reading the Leningrad Codex, Folio 12v–13r:**
Genesis 23 occupies the latter portion of folio 12v (page 1 of the chapter source images) and the opening portion of folio 13r (page 2). The folio 12v image is photographed inverted; once oriented, the right column clearly carries the conclusion of Genesis 22 (the Nahor genealogy, vv. 20–24), while the left column opens Genesis 23 with a *samekh* (ס) marker — a closed section (*parashah setumah*) — indicating a minor division at the chapter boundary. This is notable: the transition from the Aqedah narrative and genealogy to Sarah's death is marked as a *closed* section rather than an *open* one (*parashah petuchah*, marked *pe*), suggesting the Masoretic editors regarded chapters 22 and 23 as more tightly connected than they might appear. Folio 13r (the second source image, correctly oriented) carries Genesis 23 from approximately v. 14 through v. 20 and proceeds into the opening of Genesis 24. The three-column Leningrad Codex layout is readable throughout; the Masoretic cantillation marks (*te'amim*) and vowel points (*nikud*) are clearly visible in the manuscript hand. The Vaticanus pages 18–19 carry the Greek LXX of this chapter in uncial *scriptura continua*: page 18 (online 0022) bears the heading *CAP. XXV* and begins the chapter from approximately Sarah's death, with the right-margin notation *CAP. IIII* visible; page 19 (online 0023) completes the chapter and begins Genesis 24, with a marginal *CAP. XXVII* visible at the left.

**23:1 — The Tripartite Age Formula and Its Echo:**
The age of Sarah at death is given in an unusual, tripartite form: *mē'āh šānāh wĕ'eśrîm šānāh wĕšeba' šānîm* — "a hundred years and twenty years and seven years." This is not the normal way Hebrew counts years in the patriarchal narratives (contrast Abraham's age at 175 in 25:7: *mē'āh šānāh wĕšib'îm šānāh wăḥāmēš šānîm*). The separation of the years into three additive units — each with its own *šānāh/šānîm* ("year/years") — is deliberate. Rashi's classical reading holds that each segment is grammatically complete so that each may be evaluated morally: at one hundred she was as free of sin as at twenty (since below twenty one is not liable in the heavenly court), and at twenty she was as beautiful as at seven. Whether or not one accepts the midrashic interpretation, the grammatical form demands notice — the repetition of the measurement word with each segment is unusual and must not be smoothed over in translation. The verse closes with the resumptive clause *šĕnê ḥayyê śārāh* — "the years of the life of Sarah" — an echo of the same formula used for Abraham at 25:7 and Isaac at 35:28, and for Sarah's father figure in the *tôledôt* lists. This echo-close functions as a formal epitaph: her life is summed, bracketed, complete. I have preserved both the tripartite structure and the echo-close.

**23:2 — "Kiriath-arba — which is Hebron" (*Qiryat 'Arba' hî' Ḥebrôn*):**
The gloss *hî' Ḥebrôn* ("which is Hebron") is the narrator's explanatory identification for the reader, linking the archaic name *Qiryat 'Arba'* (literally "city of four" or "city of Arba") with the name known to later audiences. *'Arba'* means "four" in Hebrew; the name may refer to four clans, four mountains, or a person named Arba — identified at Josh 14:15 as "the greatest man among the Anakim." The identification formula *hî' X* appears throughout Genesis as a geographic gloss (cf. 14:2, 14:7, 14:8 etc.), consistently preserved in EMET translation. The LXX Vaticanus reads *Polis Arbōk* (Πόλις Αρβωκ) for Kiriath-arba and then *hautē estin Chebrōn* (αὕτη ἐστὶν Χεβρών) — "this is Hebron" — preserving the gloss structure. Note that Sarah dies in *Canaan* — in the land promised but not yet owned — and she will be buried there. The purchase of the cave is therefore not merely a funeral transaction but the acquisition of the first concrete parcel of the promised land, Abraham's first legal title in Canaan.

**23:2 — "To weep for her" (*wĕlivkōtāh*, וְלִבְכֹּתָהּ) — The Small Kaf:**
The Masoretic scribal tradition marks the *kaf* (כ) in *livkōtāh* as an unusually small letter — a *kaf qetannah*. This scribal diminution is noted in the masorah parva and has generated considerable comment. The most common interpretation: Abraham's weeping was restrained, measured — the grief of a man of faith who accepts God's decree, not the uncontrolled weeping of one without hope. Others suggest the smallness reflects the historical reality that Sarah had already lived a complete life (*v. 1's epitaph clause*) and that Abraham's grief, while real, was balanced by that completeness. Whatever the intention of the original scribes, the small letter is visually present on the manuscript page and cannot be noted in a running translation — I note it here. The verbal structure of v. 2 is precise: first *lisĕpōd* (לִסְפֹּד, "to mourn," the formal lamentation ritual) then *wĕlivkōtāh* ("and to weep for her") — the communal ritual of mourning precedes the private act of weeping, or perhaps they are sequential stages of the same grief.

**23:3 — "Arose from before his dead" (*mē'al pĕnê mētô*, מֵעַל פְּנֵי מֵתוֹ):**
The phrase *mē'al pĕnê mētô* — "from upon the face of his dead [one]" — is a striking spatial idiom. Abraham has been *sitting* or *prostrate* over the body of his wife — the Hebrew *mē'al pĕnê* implies physical closeness, presence at the face of the body. When he "arises" (*wayyāqom*), it is the movement from grief to action, from mourning's paralysis to the practical necessity of burial in a land that is not yet his. The same verb *qūm* ("to arise") will punctuate this chapter: Abraham arises (v. 3), the property "arises" as a confirmed transaction (vv. 17, 20). The word becomes a quiet structural verb of the chapter — arising from grief, the property arising into legal title.

**23:4 — *Ger ve-toshav* (גֵּר-וְתוֹשָׁב) — "Sojourner and Resident":**
This paired term is Abraham's self-designation before the Hittites and is one of the most legally and theologically loaded expressions in the chapter. *Gēr* (גֵּר) denotes a sojourner, a foreigner resident temporarily in a land not his own, without native land-rights. *Tôšāb* (תּוֹשָׁב) denotes a settler or permanent resident — someone who has established residence, a denizen. The pairing *gēr-wĕtôšāb* is paradoxical: Abraham claims the status of both — foreigner *and* resident — simultaneously. He is making a nuanced legal claim: he lives among them permanently (*tôšāb*) but holds no hereditary land rights (*gēr*). Legally he cannot own land in Canaan under normal ANE conventions — hence the purchase negotiation that follows. The phrase is not merely social positioning; it is a legal formula establishing his status at the city gate. The tension between *gēr* and *tôšāb* runs through the entire Pentateuch: Israel will be commanded to treat the *gēr* with justice because "you were sojourners (*gērîm*) in Egypt" (Lev 19:34). The LXX Vaticanus renders the pair as *paroikos kai parepidēmos* (πάροικος καὶ παρεπίδημος) — "one dwelling alongside [others]" and "a temporary resident" — both carrying the foreigner-without-rights connotation. These same two words appear at 1 Pet 2:11 (*paroikous kai parepidēmous*) applied to Christians as people of the coming age who are foreigners in the present world — a direct theological echo of Abraham's self-description.

**23:6 — *Nesi' Elohim* (נְשִׂיא אֱלֹהִים) — "A Prince of God" / "A Mighty Prince":**
The Hittites address Abraham as *nĕśî' 'ĕlōhîm* — literally "a prince/chieftain of God." The term *nāśî'* (נָשִׂיא) is a leader, a raised one, a prince or chief — specifically one who has been elevated (*nāśā'*, "to lift up"). The genitive construction *nĕśî' 'ĕlōhîm* is debated: (a) "a prince *of God*" — a divinely-appointed or divinely-recognized chieftain; (b) a superlative use of the divine name — "a *mighty* prince," the greatest of princes (the same construction as *harrê 'ĕl*, "mountains of God" = mighty mountains, Ps 36:7). Modern translations tend to render it as the superlative "a mighty prince" (ESV, NASB, NIV), reading *'ĕlōhîm* as an intensifier. But the literal reading "prince of God" (EMET: I preserve the literal) is equally valid and the theological resonance is significant — the Hittites recognize Abraham's status as one who stands in relation to the divine. The LXX Vaticanus reads *basileus para theou* (βασιλεὺς παρὰ θεοῦ) — "a king from God" — a strong, theologically explicit rendering that removes the ambiguity by reading *'ĕlōhîm* as "God" not as an intensifier. I translate the literal "prince of God" and note the alternative.

**23:6 — "In the choicest of our burial places" (*bĕmivchar qĕvārênû*):**
The Hittite offer is generous in its surface form — but Abraham does not accept it. He wants a *specific* cave: the cave of Machpelah. The Hittite offer of any and all burial sites underscores the social elevation they accord him, but Abraham's insistence on a defined, purchased, *owned* site signals his purpose. He is not simply seeking a place to bury Sarah — he is acquiring ancestral property in the promised land, establishing the legal foundation for inheritance. The contrast between the Hittites' open-handed offer (any of our graves) and Abraham's narrow request (this particular cave) is the rhetorical and theological center of the negotiation.

**23:7 — "Bowed down to the people of the land" (*wayyishtachû lĕ'am-hā'āreṣ*):**
The verb *hishtaḥăwāh* (הִשְׁתַּחֲוָה) is a full prostration bow — the same word used for worshipful prostration before God (Gen 22:5). Abraham prostrates himself *twice* in this chapter: once at v. 7 (to the Hittites generally) and once at v. 12 (specifically to Ephron and the people of the land). This is not worship; it is the formal negotiation posture of a petitioner before a court. ANE legal transactions were conducted at the city gate before witnesses, with formal bowing signaling the supplicant's acknowledgment of the community's authority to validate the transaction. Abraham, who owns nothing in Canaan, bows before those who do. The irony — that the heir of the divine promise bows before Hittites to buy a cave — is not suppressed in the text. It is part of the chapter's sober realism: the promise is real, but its fulfillment begins with a single cave purchased with weighed silver.

**23:8 — "If it is in your soul" (*'im-yēš 'et-napšĕkem*):**
The phrase *'im-yēš 'et-napšĕkem liqqĕbōr* is a polite conditional: "if it is with [i.e., agreeable to] your soul/desire to bury..." The word *nefesh* (נֶפֶשׁ) here functions as the seat of desire or will — "if it is your wish/desire." This is the vocabulary of diplomatic indirection: Abraham does not command or demand — he frames his request as contingent on their willingness. The rhetorical layer of elaborate courtesy masks the determination underneath. The word *pāga'* (פָּגַע, "intercede, entreat, meet") used for "intercede for me" is interesting — it is used elsewhere for hostile encounter (e.g., falling upon someone, 1 Kgs 2:25) and for intercessory prayer (Isa 53:12, "he made intercession for the transgressors"). Here it means: "approach/plead with Ephron on my behalf." The diplomatic chain is: Abraham addresses the Hittites collectively; they will approach Ephron; Ephron then responds directly. This is ANE protocol — the intermediary role of the community.

**23:9 — *Me'arat ha-Makhpelah* (מְעָרַת הַמַּכְפֵּלָה) — "The Cave of Machpelah":**
The name *ha-Makhpelah* (הַמַּכְפֵּלָה) is almost certainly derived from the root *kāfal* (כָּפַל, "to fold, double, duplicate") — the *makhpelah* is "the doubled [cave]," a cave with two chambers, or perhaps a double cave cut into a hillside. The definite article (*ha-*) suggests an already-known designation: this cave had a name before Abraham purchased it. The traditional site is identified with the Cave of Machpelah in Hebron, today enclosed within the Ibrahimi Mosque / Cave of Machpelah complex — a site venerated by Jews, Muslims, and Christians as the burial place of the patriarchs and matriarchs. The LXX renders the name as *to spēlaion to diploun* (τὸ σπήλαιον τὸ διπλοῦν) — "the double cave" — explicitly glossing the etymology. I preserve the Hebrew name *Makhpelah* with the transliterated article-form and note the double-cave etymology.

**23:9 — "For the full silver" (*bĕkesef mālē'*):**
Abraham specifies payment terms before the negotiation has even engaged Ephron directly: he will pay *the full price* — *kesef mālē'* (כֶּסֶף מָלֵא), literally "silver fullness" or "full silver." This preemptive declaration that he will pay in full is significant: he wants no ambiguity, no gift, no partial claim, no future dispute about ownership. The cave must be purchased outright so that the burial place — and therefore the patriarchal stake in Canaan — is legally unambiguous. The phrase *bĕtôkĕkem* (בְּתוֹכְכֶם, "in your midst") marks that the community serves as witness to the transaction.

**23:10 — "All the entering-ones of the gate of his city" (*kol-bā'ê ša'ar-'îrô*):**
The phrase *bā'ê ša'ar* (those who enter/come to the gate) is the technical term for the citizens assembled at the city gate — the legal assembly, the court. In ANE culture the city gate was simultaneously the commercial market, the judicial court, and the civic assembly point. A transaction conducted "before all the entering-ones of the gate" was a public, legally witnessed act with maximal force. Ephron, who "was sitting in the midst of the sons of Heth" (*yōšēb bĕtôk bĕnê-ḥēt*), is a member of this gate-assembly — he holds legal standing to transact. This phrase is repeated at v. 18 as the formal witness formula closing the transaction: the purchase is confirmed before the same gate-community that opened it. I render *bā'ê ša'ar* literally as "entering-ones of the gate" rather than smoothing it to "citizens" or "elders," preserving the gate imagery central to the chapter's legal architecture.

**23:11 — Ephron's triple "I give" (*nātattî... nĕtattîhā... nĕtattîhā*):**
Ephron's speech uses the verb *nātan* ("to give") three times in a single verse — *nātattî lāk... nĕtattîhā... nĕtattîhā lāk*. The rhetorical force is generous benefaction — triple affirmation of the gift — yet it is precisely this theatrically generous offer that Abraham will not accept. The field is offered along with the cave (*field and cave*), not only the cave Abraham requested. Ephron is expanding the transaction — whether from generosity or from commercial calculation (getting Abraham to accept more property at a higher price) is left ambiguous. The "gift" is not accepted; in v. 13 Abraham insists on paying. The entire scene is a masterpiece of ANE negotiation protocol: what looks like a gift offer is the opening gambit in a price negotiation. Accepting the gift would leave Abraham socially and legally indebted; payment is the only path to unencumbered title.

**23:13 — "Surely if only you" (*'ak 'im-'attāh lû šĕma'ĕnî*):**
The particle sequence *'ak 'im-'attāh* is grammatically compressed and rhetorically forceful — something like "but surely, if *you* [will listen to me]." The *'ak* (אַךְ) is an asseverative or restrictive particle ("surely/only/but"), and the *'im* introduces the conditional. The phrase is Abraham's polite but firm counter-move: he acknowledges Ephron's offer but insists on redirecting it toward a cash purchase. The verb *nātatî kesef ha-śādeh* — "I give the silver of the field" — uses a perfect of certainty: the silver *is given*, or *shall certainly be given*. Abraham's language is as assertive as politeness permits. The LXX Vaticanus renders this verse with *ei su ei* (εἰ σύ εἶ) — "if you are [willing]" — smoothing the abruptness somewhat.

**23:15 — "Four hundred shekels of silver, passing [currency] for the merchant" (*'arba' mē'ôt šeqel-kesef 'ōvēr la-sōḥēr*, עֹבֵר לַסֹּחֵר):**
The weight formula *'ōvēr la-sōḥēr* is a commercial technical term: literally "passing to/for the merchant" or "current with the merchant." Silver in the ancient Near East was not yet minted coin — it was weighed in shekel-weight units and the phrase *'ōvēr la-sōḥēr* indicates silver that has been assayed and is recognized as standard weight by the trading community, i.e., commercially acceptable currency. The *sōḥēr* (סֹחֵר, "merchant, trader, one who goes about [trading]") was the professional commercial class; their acceptance of the silver as *'ōvēr* ("passing," "current") certified its purity and weight. 400 shekels is a very large sum — by comparison, Jeremiah purchases a field for 17 shekels (Jer 32:9); David buys the threshing floor of Araunah for 50 shekels of silver (2 Sam 24:24). The high price may reflect Ephron's commercial acumen, the political leverage of Abraham's need, and/or the symbolic weight of the transaction — the first patriarchal land purchase in Canaan. Ephron names the price by framing it as almost nothing ("what is that between me and you?") — the minimizing language of the skilled negotiator. I preserve *'ōvēr la-sōḥēr* in transliteration in the main text and explain here.

**23:16 — "Abraham weighed out" (*wayyišqōl 'abrāhām*, וַיִּשְׁקֹל):**
The verb *šāqal* (שָׁקַל, "to weigh") is the root of the noun *šeqel* (שֶׁקֶל) — a shekel is literally "a weighed [unit]." Abraham *weighs* out the silver on a balance scale; this is the physical act of pre-coinage commerce. The weighing is done "in the hearing of the sons of Heth" (*bĕ'oznê bĕnê-ḥēt*) — audible, witnessed, formally announced. The legal redundancy — heard and witnessed — is characteristic of ANE real estate law. The formula "in the hearing of" (*bĕ'oznê*) appears repeatedly in this chapter (vv. 10, 13, 16) as the formal witness frame.

**23:17–18 — "Arose/Confirmed" (*wayyāqom*, וַיָּקָם) — The Legal Formula:**
The verb *qûm* in vv. 17 and 20 — rendered literally as "arose" — functions as a legal formula of confirmation and establishment. In ANE real estate transfer language, a property "arising" (*qūm*) to a buyer means it is established, confirmed, legally vested in the new owner. The same root is used for covenants being "confirmed" or "established" (Gen 6:18, 17:7 etc.). The legal deed of the chapter is framed by two *wayyāqom* statements: v. 17 lists all the elements of the property, v. 18 names the new owner (Abraham) and the witnesses (the gate assembly). The transaction is complete. I render *wayyāqom* literally as "arose [confirmed]" with the bracketed gloss to preserve both the literal form and its legal sense.

**23:17 — The Property Inventory:**
The property transfer formula in v. 17 is one of the most carefully constructed legal inventories in the Torah: (1) the field of Ephron at Machpelah; (2) before Mamre; (3) the field; (4) the cave in it; (5) every tree in the field; (6) in all its boundary round about. This exhaustive enumeration — field, cave, trees, and all boundaries — is standard ANE real estate deeding language. Trees were separately valuable property and their specific inclusion confirms that the entire real property, not merely the cave, has transferred. Comparable formulaic property descriptions appear in Mesopotamian land purchase documents. The care of the formula contrasts with the apparently casual negotiation that preceded it — the rhetorical courtesy of vv. 3–16 resolves into precise legal language in vv. 17–18.

**23:19 — "Over the face of Mamre" (*'al-pĕnê mamrē'*, עַל-פְּנֵי מַמְרֵא):**
The preposition *'al-pĕnê* means literally "upon the face of" — i.e., "facing," "opposite," "before." The cave is located *in front of* the terebinths of Mamre. Mamre is the place where Abraham received the three divine visitors (Gen 18) and learned of the divine promise of Isaac — the place of promise is also now the place of death and burial. The cave of Machpelah before Mamre becomes the patriarchal burial site: Sarah (23:19), Abraham (25:9), Isaac and Rebekah, and Jacob and Leah (49:31, 50:13). The land of promise is first entered by the dead.

**23:20 — The Closing Formula — Sealed Possession:**
Verse 20 is the formal closing summary of the transaction: both field *and* cave pass to Abraham as *'ăḥuzzat-qāber* (אֲחֻזַּת-קֶבֶר, "holding/possession of a burial-place") — the same phrase used in Abraham's original request at v. 4. The legal term *'ăḥuzzāh* (אֲחֻזָּה, "holding, possession, hereditary property") is drawn from the root *'āḥaz* (אָחַז, "to seize, grasp, hold"). This is property that is *held*, *grasped*, owned — not borrowed, not a gift that might be reclaimed. The repetition of the exact phrase from v. 4 (*'ăḥuzzat-qāber*) closes the chapter as a ring: what Abraham requested at the outset is confirmed at the end in precisely the same language.

**LXX Vaticanus Observations (Pages 18–19):**
The Vaticanus LXX text of Genesis 23 is carried across pages 18–19 of the manuscript (online 0022–0023). The chapter heading *CAP. XXV* in the ancient Greek chapter enumeration appears at the top of page 18 — the ancient Greek chapter divisions do not align with the modern Hebrew chapter numbering. The chapter is transmitted in three columns of *scriptura continua* (no word spaces) in a careful 4th-century uncial hand. Most significant LXX variants for this chapter:

1. **23:1** — LXX reads *egeneto de hē zōē Sarras* (ἐγένετο δὲ ἡ ζωὴ Σαρρας) — "and the life of Sarah was..." — without repeating "the years of the life of Sarah" at verse end. The Greek omits the echo-close (*šĕnê ḥayyê śārāh*) found in the Masoretic text. The echo-close appears present in the Hebrew but is not mirrored in LXX Vaticanus — this is a case where the LXX may reflect a shorter Hebrew Vorlage, or the translator simply avoided redundancy.

2. **23:6** — LXX reads *basileus para theou* (βασιλεὺς παρὰ θεοῦ) for *nĕśî' 'ĕlōhîm* — "a king from God" rather than "a prince of God." This is an interpretive elevation: Abraham is not merely a tribal chief but a king in the LXX's framing, and the divine source of his status is made explicit. The MT's *nāśî'* ("prince/chieftain") is a less hierarchically defined term than *basileus* ("king").

3. **23:10** — LXX *Ephron de ekathēto* (Εφρων δὲ ἐκάθητο) — "Ephron was sitting" — matches the Hebrew *yōšēb* precisely.

4. **23:16** — LXX does not render *'ōvēr la-sōḥēr* (the commercial weight standard phrase) with a matching technical equivalent — the Greek *nomimon emporois* (νόμιμον ἐμπόροις) — "lawful/accepted among merchants" — captures the sense ("valid commercial currency") though *nomimon* (lawful, standard) is interpretive rather than literal.

5. **23:17–18** — The LXX property inventory formula is close to the MT but renders the closing *wayyāqom* confirmation verb as *estē* (ἔστη, "it stood/was established") — the same legal-confirmation sense I have rendered "arose [confirmed]."

**DSS Note:**
Per the chapter manifest, no significant Genesis 23 fragments are extant among the Dead Sea Scrolls. The Leningrad Codex and Codex Vaticanus are therefore the sole manuscript witnesses examined for this chapter.
